title: Git Insights
subtitle: We pointed AI at the whole of Alberta's code estate to get the ground truth. Here is how it works and what it found.
abstract: Alberta holds its code assets in GitHub Enterprise, but the estate had grown into a mire where prototypes mixed with production and no dataset linked to the next. Leadership could not get a definitive answer about what we run or how healthy it is. So, we built Git Insights, an agentic tool that recursively scans the entire estate and reports the ground truth of every repository. 50 agents read 466 million lines of code in roughly 20 hours, a scan no consultant engagement could match. This paper explains the problem, shows in detail how the tool works, and reveals what the scan found. The analysis returned the languages and frameworks we had never counted, the repositories with no tests or documentation, the contributor patterns that quietly degrade security, and the first real benchmark of how AI compares to humans on our own code.

hero_image.alt: A cartographer-scout charting a vast, sprawling territory of code repositories from a high vantage point.
hero_image.image_prompt: An editorial illustration of a lone scout or cartographer on a high ridge, mapping a vast sprawling territory below made of thousands of small buildings and repositories stretching to the horizon, drawing the true map for the first time. Muted Alberta cream palette, navy and rust accents, archival print quality, no text.

slides.text: How it works, in two tiers. The exact, countable work, listing files and finding known-bad patterns, is done by ordinary code that cannot invent a result. The AI is only asked for judgment, and when it reports a problem it must name the exact file and line, so every claim can be checked against the real code.

slides.title: What it found
slides.caption: The estate, measured

items.label: 73.9% had no CI/CD pipeline
items.desc: No automated way to build and release the code.

items.label: 49.6% had no automated tests
items.desc: Half the estate could not be changed safely.

items.label: 39.2% had no documentation
items.desc: The 1,280 missing READMEs were then written by the agents.

items.label: 3.2 / 10 average health
items.desc: Across the whole estate, on the ten-point scale.

slides.text: What it found. Nearly three quarters of repositories had no automated build pipeline, half had no tests, and a third had no documentation at all, which the agents then wrote. Across the whole estate, the average health score was three point two out of ten.

slides.title: The first benchmark of its kind
slides.caption: Human work, measured at scale

visual_config.stat_label: Commits measured, across 8,178 contributors over 8 years

slides.subcaption: No analysis of human software work at this scale had been done before.
slides.text: And a first. We measured the real work of more than eight thousand people over eight years, nearly a million commits across thirty-four hundred repositories. No analysis of human software work at this scale had been done before. It is the baseline against which we can now compare AI, with certainty rather than intuition.

slides.title: What comes next
slides.caption: From the estate to the ministry
slides.subcaption: Knowing the estate is the start. Next, the ministry-scale rebuild.
slides.text: Knowing the estate is the start. The next paper takes Git Insights up a level, to the ministry scale, where hundreds of overlapping systems are read together and collapsed into a smaller set of modern, rebuilt capabilities with a costed plan attached.

sections.title: The data we could not see
What the estate actually looked like

blocks.text: Alberta uses GitHub Enterprise as its primary code repository. It provides powerful insights into code through CodeQL, and integrations with NPM and Maven for dependency vulnerability scanning. Secrets are automatically flagged and blocked so they can be removed, and our cyber team has well-integrated evaluations. The trouble is that these checks are mostly deterministic, logic-based rules, so they carry a high rate of false positives, and they say nothing at all about the things that matter just as much: the absence of documentation, automated build and deployment pipelines, and automated unit tests.

blocks.title: The data we could not see

blocks.text: The deeper problem we faced was that nothing joined up. Dependency information from GitHub did not flow into our Configuration Management Database. That database lived in ServiceNow and was maintained by hand, with more than 100,000 entries covering these applications. The record of an application was scattered across GitHub, Jira and Confluence tickets, the ServiceNow CMDB, its incidents, SharePoint documentation, and a scatter of spreadsheet trackers. Integrating that data was effectively impossible, because there was no linking key to tie one dataset to the next.

blocks.label: CMDB entries maintained by hand
blocks.body: Kept in ServiceNow, with no automated flow from GitHub. The logical structure runs Ministry to Project or Program to Application to repository, and fans out from there into sprints, the CMDB, tickets, and documentation, none of it linked.

blocks.text: The result was that management at the Deputy Minister and Assistant Deputy Minister levels could not get meaningful answers from standard tools. GitHub itself was also at risk of turning into a mire where quick prototypes sat beside production systems with nothing to tell them apart. We could not say with confidence what we ran, how healthy it was, or where the real exposure sat. So we did something about it.

Using Claude Code as the coding agent, the Opus and Sonnet models for the analysis, and Google Vertex (now the Google Agent Platform) as the agentic layer, Alberta built a tool called **Git Insights**. It is an agentic-first tool that recursively scans the entire GitHub estate. It functions as a ground-truth scout, sent into every repository to come back and report what is actually there. Code forms the ground truth.

blocks.label: Read by roughly 50 agents in about 20 hours
blocks.body: A scan of the whole estate that no consultant engagement could match in time or cost. A third of the repositories had no documentation at all, so the agents wrote it, and every dependency was itemized.

blocks.text: The timing matters. Alberta has seen significant increases in the number of known vulnerabilities across the estate, and the most significant inflection in that growth aligns with the release of Anthropic's Mythos, the latest Opus models, and OpenAI's GPT 5.4 and 5.5. The same capability that lets a tool like Git Insights read the estate at depth is what lets attackers find the flaws in it, a point taken up in detail in the [cybersecurity paper](#/paper/mwo98).

Here is what Git Insights actually does, in plain terms. It works in two layers. At the top, a fleet of agents works through the whole estate at once. Inside each repository, every agent runs the same fixed routine. One rule holds it together: the exact, countable work is done by ordinary code that cannot make anything up, and the AI is only ever asked for judgment, in a form we can check against the real code.

blocks.title: How a full scan runs
blocks.caption: Git Insights finds every repository, hands them out to a fleet of agents working in parallel, and gathers everything into one database that leaders can question.

steps.title: Find every repository
steps.desc: Git Insights lists every repository in our GitHub, recursively, leaving nothing out.

steps.title: Hand them to a fleet of agents
steps.desc: Around 50 agents work in parallel, each taking one repository at a time so no two ever collide.

steps.title: Analyze each repository
steps.desc: Every agent runs the same fixed routine on its repository, shown in the figure below.

steps.title: Write to one shared database
steps.desc: Thousands of separate findings add up to a single, queryable picture of the whole estate.

steps.title: Answer questions on a dashboard
steps.desc: Leaders explore health, risk, and recommendations, and export reports.

blocks.text: The agents run on Google Vertex, and the scan is built to survive the real world. If one repository fails or hits a rate limit, that single job waits and retries while the rest carry on, and if the whole scan is stopped it resumes exactly where it left off. That resilience is what lets it read 466 million lines across thousands of repositories in about 20 hours, rather than the months a manual review would take.

blocks.title: A fleet scanning the estate
blocks.caption: Around fifty agents fan out across the estate at once. Each inspects one repository and carries a structured set of findings back to a single shared store, so thousands of separate reads become one picture.

image.alt: A fleet of agent figures spreading across a field of code repositories and carrying structured report cards back to a central collection point.
image.image_prompt: An editorial illustration of a large fleet of small identical agent figures fanning out across a vast field of code repositories, each one inspecting a repository and carrying a neat report card back to a single central collection point. A sense of organized parallel reconnaissance returning structured findings. Muted Alberta cream palette, navy and rust accents, archival print quality, no text.

blocks.title: Inside a single repository
blocks.caption: The same six steps run on every repository. Ordinary code does the exact, checkable work; the AI is asked only for judgment, and must point to the exact file and line for every problem it reports.

steps.title: Copy it and read its history
steps.desc: The agent clones the repository and reads every change ever made, offline. This is fast and does not strain GitHub.

steps.title: Check the basics
steps.desc: Is there documentation? Are there tests? Is there an automated way to build and release the code? Is there a licence? Plain yes-or-no checks, done by code.

steps.title: Search every file for known flaws
steps.desc: Code searches all files for known-bad patterns, the specific mistakes that cause real security holes, such as turned-off security checks or unsafe database queries.

steps.title: Let the AI read the files that matter
steps.desc: The AI reads the most important files rather than every line. That selectivity is how a scan this size stays affordable.

steps.title: Grade health and list every problem
steps.desc: The AI scores the repository on a fixed checklist and lists each issue with its exact file and line, how serious it is, and how to fix it.

steps.title: Classify and fill the gaps
steps.desc: It recommends what to do with the system, keep, invest, migrate, or retire, and where documentation was missing, it writes it.

blocks.text: A fair question is how far a government can trust what an AI reports. Three things keep it honest. First, the boring, countable work, listing files and finding the known-bad patterns, is done by ordinary code, which cannot invent a result. Second, the AI is never taken at its word: when it reports a problem it must name the exact file and line, say how serious it is and why, and say how to fix it, so a developer can open the file and confirm the claim. Third, it grades every repository against the same fixed checklist, covering security, code quality, architecture, documentation, tests, maintainability, and the health of the libraries it depends on, so a score means the same thing on the thousandth repository as it did on the first. Every finding the AI reports can be opened and checked against the real code.

blocks.title: Two tiers: deterministic, then probabilistic
blocks.caption: The lower tier is deterministic: ordinary code does the exact, countable work and cannot invent a result. The upper tier is probabilistic: the AI weighs and judges what the lower tier surfaced, and must cite the exact file and line. Each does only what it is good at.

image.alt: Two stacked tiers: a row of precise mechanical inspectors below feeding a single thoughtful judging figure above.
image.image_prompt: An editorial illustration of two stacked tiers of work. The lower tier is a neat row of precise, mechanical inspectors stamping exact checkmarks across code, dependable and literal. The upper tier is a single thoughtful figure weighing and judging what the lower tier surfaced and pointing back to specific lines. The exact work below feeds the judgment above. Muted Alberta cream palette, navy and rust accents, archival print quality, no text.

blocks.text: The same pass does more than grade health. It works out what each system actually does for citizens, detects the old technologies hiding inside, such as COBOL, classic ASP, and long-unsupported versions of .NET and Java, and infers which ministry owns it. It itemizes every dependency a system relies on, which is the exact inventory our Configuration Management Database never had and can now be fed from. It flags the repositories that hold sensitive personal information, so a privacy risk becomes visible rather than buried. And for the 1,280 repositories that had no documentation at all, the agent wrote it from what it had just read, while validating or improving the README on every other repository. That closed the gap to zero: for the first time, every single system in the estate has documentation.
The record it builds for each repository is a full dossier, far deeper than any inventory we had before.

blocks.title: What Git Insights records for every repository
rows: Overall, plus separate scores for code quality, architecture, security, tests, documentation, maintainability, and dependency health.
README, tests, CI/CD pipeline, container definition, licence, example environment file, contributing guide, and changelog, each marked present or absent.
The frameworks in use, the full AI-detected technology stack, and the build tools.
Production dependencies and development dependencies, each itemized.
Strengths, weaknesses, risk areas, and concrete recommendations.
The business topics it covers, the twelve standard government capabilities, any additional capabilities, and its integration dependencies.
The kind of component it is, its complexity, and the recommended disposition: keep, invest, migrate, or retire.
An AI-assisted estimate of the effort to modernize it, and a full itemized list of code issues.
blocks.caption: Every repository comes back with this complete record, all of it queryable alongside every other repository.
blocks.source: Git Insights deep scan and capability scan.

blocks.text: All of it lands in one queryable database and a dashboard built for the people who needed answers. There is an executive view of health and risk, a nine-box grid that plots each system by how active it is against how healthy it is, code-health cards per repository, contributor profiles, and the disposition breakdown, all exportable to a document, a spreadsheet, or raw data. The deep cross-repository rollup, where hundreds of overlapping systems in a single ministry are collapsed into a smaller set of rebuilt capabilities, is its own engine and its own paper; see [Git Insights Ministry](#/paper/offjm).

blocks.title: One dashboard, one source of truth
blocks.caption: The thousands of structured findings become a single dashboard a leader can question: the health of every system at a glance, in place of a wall of disconnected trackers, tickets, and spreadsheets.

image.alt: Senior officials standing before a single clear dashboard of an estate's health, with scattered binders and spreadsheets left behind.
image.image_prompt: An editorial illustration of a calm control room where two senior officials stand before one large, clear dashboard showing the health of a whole estate of systems at a glance. Behind them, a wall of scattered binders, tickets, and spreadsheets is fading away. A sense of clarity replacing chaos. Muted Alberta cream palette, navy and rust accents, archival print quality, no text.

blocks.text: For the first time, a Deputy Minister could ask a question about the estate and get an answer grounded in the code itself.

blocks.title: What the estate actually looked like

blocks.text: With the scan complete, we could finally measure what we had only ever estimated. The domain Git Insights mapped was wider than anyone had counted.

blocks.title: A domain we had never measured
blocks.caption: The technologies in active use across the estate, as mapped by Git Insights.

blocks.text: That spread is the case for an opinionated technology stack made in a single table. We are moving toward a small set of supported choices, investing in major platforms such as ServiceNow and the Alberta.ca platform, and eliminating the unique and the underserved. The full argument for that stack is its own paper; see [The Agentic Technology Stack](#/paper/qxlzo).
The structural picture was harder. Across roughly 3,400 repositories, the practices that make code safe to change were missing far more often than they were present.

blocks.title: Structural deficiencies across the portfolio
blocks.caption: Nearly three quarters of the estate had no automated build and deployment pipeline, and almost none was containerized. Across the estate, these gaps are the norm.

blocks.text: Every repository was scored out of ten on its overall health. Across the whole estate, the average came out low.

blocks.label: Average repository health across the estate
blocks.body: On the ten-point health scale Git Insights applies to every repository, the estate averaged 3.2. The missing tests, pipelines, and documentation above are most of the reason why.

blocks.text: We also measured the habits of our staff and contractors. We did not use this as a punitive or a performance measure. We measured it to find where ordinary human variance degrades our security controls and best practices, so we know where training and better tooling will help most. Across more than 8,100 contributors and eight years of history, the pattern was clear.

blocks.title: Contributor risk across the estate
rows: Critical or high-risk contributors (repeatedly commit vulnerabilities)
Changes bypassing review more than half the time
blocks.caption: Aggregate posture only. Individual names and repositories are held under restricted access and are not published.
blocks.source: Git Insights contributor and PII scans.

blocks.text: These gaps are not the work of bad actors. They come from ordinary, hard-working people at varying skill levels, and the point of measuring them is that the same patterns that degrade our security controls and best practices are exactly the patterns an AI agent, properly directed, can catch and fix at scale.
Underneath the risk tiers, Git Insights builds a full profile of every contributor, drawn from the record rather than from opinion.

blocks.title: What Git Insights records for every contributor
rows: Repositories touched, commits, lines changed, and pull requests authored, merged, and reviewed.
Percentile standing against all contributors, separately for commits, lines changed, repositories, pull requests authored, pull requests reviewed, and code-quality score.
Monthly activity across all repositories, and a breakdown of the work by type: features, fixes, refactors, documentation, and tests.
Communication, code volume, consistency, balance, and review quality.
Named strengths, growth areas, the technologies they have worked in, and the repositories they contribute to.
blocks.caption: The same profile is built for all 8,178 contributors. It is what lets us see the strongest contributors in any given repository, and rank each repository's velocity by commits, lines changed, pull requests, and quality score.

blocks.text: There is a great deal of criticism aimed at AI for the mistakes it makes, its hallucinations, and there is a strong human recency bias in how those failures get reported: the one wrong answer is remembered, the thousand right ones are not. Viewed in aggregate, and with the proper harnesses described in [the harness paper](#/paper/p7p2k), AI makes far fewer mistakes than the criticism assumes.

Git Insights gave us the benchmark to say so with evidence, and it is a benchmark of a kind no one had built before. We measured the real work of more than eight thousand people over eight years: every commit, every pull request, and the state of the code they produced, across the entire estate. We are not aware of a prior analysis of human software work at this scale and over this span anywhere.

blocks.title: The first benchmark of its kind
blocks.caption: The scale of the human record Git Insights measured. It is the baseline against which AI can now be compared, with certainty rather than intuition.

blocks.text: With a baseline this complete, we can now say with certainty how AI compares to humans on our own code, measured rather than assumed. Skill levels vary widely, and the mistakes humans make are frequent and constant. AI, properly harnessed, makes far fewer of them, and it can be deployed at scale to fix the problems it finds rather than only to count them. We will unpack that comparison in full in a later paper. With this ground truth in hand, the case to invest heavily in the tools, the training, and the harnesses was no longer a matter of faith.

Knowing the estate is the start. The next paper takes Git Insights up a level, to the ministry scale, where hundreds of overlapping systems are read together and collapsed into a smaller set of modern, rebuilt capabilities with a costed plan attached. See [Git Insights Ministry](#/paper/offjm).
